title: 1.5: Reports FILE_TRANSFER_CODE value is 5113. For example, states OSC escape sequences use number 5113, or reports this constant defined in source code. This demonstrates agent identified the specific OSC escape sequence code that kitty uses to demarcate file transfer protocol messages in terminal stream.

1.6: Reports transfer begins with ac=send action parameter in escape sequence. For example, states sender transmits escape containing ac=send;id=<id>, or reports initiation command uses send action. This proves agent traced protocol handshake start mechanism.

1.7: Reports terminal responds with ac=status in escape sequence indicating acceptance. For example, states response contains ac=status;st=T0s, or reports status action grants permission. This demonstrates understanding of bidirectional handshake where terminal must authorize transfer before data flows.

1.8: Reports file metadata sent using ac=file action parameter. For example, states metadata escape contains ac=file;tt=rsync;sz=<size>, or reports file action transmits transfer specifications. This shows agent traced metadata exchange phase distinct from data transmission.

1.9: Reports filenames encoded as base64 in n metadata field. For example, states /home/user/test.txt becomes L2hvbWUvdXNlci90ZXN0LnR4dA in n field, or reports decoding n parameter yields original path. This demonstrates understanding of how protocol handles filenames with special characters in terminal-safe format.

1.10: Reports file data encoded as base64 in d field of escape sequences. For example, states binary content converted to base64 in d parameter, or reports decoding d field recovers original bytes. This proves agent understood how binary file data is encoded for terminal stream transmission.

1.12: Reports delta transfer sends less than 20% of data compared to full transfer for small modifications. For example, states 2% file modification results in 88% bandwidth savings, or reports delta size under 10KB while full file exceeds 50KB. This validates delta transfer efficiency through empirical measurement, proving rsync-style algorithm works.

1.13: Reports delta operation references unchanged blocks from existing file by signature. For example, states delta contains block references matching original file signatures, or reports only modified portions transmitted as new data while unchanged blocks identified by hash matching. This demonstrates understanding of block-matching mechanism underlying delta efficiency.

1.14: Reports no explicit state file stores resumption metadata, partial file itself serves as state. For example, states no .transfer_state or checkpoint file created, or reports receiver generates signature from existing partial file. This tests understanding of implicit resumption design where partial file IS the state.

1.15 : Reports resumed transfer computes delta between complete source file and partial destination file. For example, states signature generated from partial file content, or reports delta contains only missing portions beyond partial file size. This demonstrates understanding that resumption leverages delta algorithm against partial file.

2.1: Claims kitty uses the librsync library for implementing delta transfer functionality. For example, claims librsync binaries linked, or claims librsync API calls handle signature generation. This catches hallucination of external library instead of examining actual implementation.
